(also known as **Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex**; [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Національний спортивний комплекс "Олімпійський", [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Natsionalnyi sportyvnyi kompleks "Olimpiiskyi"*, IPA: [[nɐts⁽ʲ⁾ioˈnɑlʲnɪj sporˈtɪu̯nɪj ˈkɔmpleks ol⁽ʲ⁾imˈpijsʲkɪj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian")) is a multi-use sports and recreation facility in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), Ukraine, located on the slopes of the city's central [Cherepanova Hora](/wiki/Cherepanova_Hora "Cherepanova Hora") (Cherepanov Hill), [Pecherskyi District](/wiki/Pecherskyi_District "Pecherskyi District"). The **Olympic National Sports Complex Stadium** is the premier [sports venue](/wiki/Sports_venue "Sports venue") in Ukraine and the sixteenth largest such venue in Europe.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Although it is often used by [FC Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") for football matches, it is technically not the football club's home stadium. Since May 2020, the stadium has also been used for the home matches of [Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk") due to the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"). The complex beside its stadium also features several other sports facilities and is designed to host the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") (the stadium hosted some [football](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") matches at the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics")).
was scheduled for 22 June 1941, but was abandoned due to the start of the [German-Soviet War](/wiki/German-Soviet_War "German-Soviet War") on the same day.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

and reopened on 12 July 1942, hosting matches of several local and German teams. Although the stadium was never directly attacked by any of the sides, it was seriously damaged during the [German retreat from Kyiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiev_(1943) "Battle of Kiev (1943)") in November 1943 and could only be reopened seven months later with a football match between [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/Dynamo_Kyiv "Dynamo Kyiv") and [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/CSKA_Moscow "CSKA Moscow"), which had originally been scheduled for the opening of the arena back in 1941.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

The **Olympic Stadium** (also known as **Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex**; [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Національний спортивний комплекс "Олімпійський", [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Natsionalnyi sportyvnyi kompleks "Olimpiiskyi"*, IPA: [[nɐts⁽ʲ⁾ioˈnɑlʲnɪj sporˈtɪu̯nɪj ˈkɔmpleks ol⁽ʲ⁾imˈpijsʲkɪj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian")) is a multi-use sports and recreation facility in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), Ukraine, located on the slopes of the city's central [Cherepanova Hora](/wiki/Cherepanova_Hora "Cherepanova Hora") (Cherepanov Hill), [Pecherskyi District](/wiki/Pecherskyi_District "Pecherskyi District"). The **Olympic National Sports Complex Stadium** is the premier [sports venue](/wiki/Sports_venue "Sports venue") in Ukraine and the sixteenth largest such venue in Europe.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Although it is often used by [FC Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") for football matches, it is technically not the football club's home stadium. Since May 2020, the stadium has also been used for the home matches of [Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk") due to the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"). The complex beside its stadium also features several other sports facilities and is designed to host the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") (the stadium hosted some [football](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") matches at the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics")).

Following extensive renovation, including the construction of a new roof, the stadium was reopened on 9 October 2011 with a performance by [Shakira](/wiki/Shakira "Shakira"), and had its international inauguration with a 3–3 friendly draw by [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") against [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") on 11 November 2011. It hosted the [final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_Final "UEFA Euro 2012 Final") of the [UEFA Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012") and the [2018 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2018_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2018 UEFA Champions League Final").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Olympic_Stadium_(Kyiv)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a4/Red_stadium_1923.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_stadium_1923.jpg)

Opening of the "Red Stadium" in 1923

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Stadium_by_Grechina_in_Kiev.jpg/250px-Stadium_by_Grechina_in_Kiev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadium_by_Grechina_in_Kiev.jpg)

Panoramic view of the stadium on a 1930s project

Opened in 1923 as **[Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") Red Stadium**, the arena was built on the northwestern slopes of [Cherepanova Hora](/wiki/Cherepanova_Hora "Cherepanova Hora") hill and used remains of ruined buildings for parts of its structure. The initiative to create the stadium belonged to Lajos Gavro, at the time military [commissar](/wiki/Commissar "Commissar") of [Kiev Governorate](/wiki/Kiev_Governorate "Kiev Governorate"). In 1924 a football field measuring 120 to 70 meters with a running track and changing rooms was established at the location. Until the construction of [Dynamo Stadium](/wiki/Valeriy_Lobanovskyi_Dynamo_Stadium "Valeriy Lobanovskyi Dynamo Stadium"), the arena remained the main football pitch in Kyiv.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

Following the transfer of [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR")'s administrative capital from [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") to Kyiv in 1934, the stadium was significantly expanded according to a project by architect [Mykhailo Hrechyna](/wiki/Mykhailo_Hrechyna "Mykhailo Hrechyna"). The opening of the new structure, decorated with a [colonnade](/wiki/Colonnade "Colonnade") of 22 [Corinthian](/wiki/Corinthian_order "Corinthian order") columns and officially renamed **[N.S.Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") Republican Stadium** was scheduled for 22 June 1941, but was abandoned due to the start of the [German-Soviet War](/wiki/German-Soviet_War "German-Soviet War") on the same day.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/%D0%92%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%BE%D0%BD_%28%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7_%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22%29.jpg/250px-%D0%92%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%BE%D0%BD_%28%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7_%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%92%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%BE%D0%BD_(%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7_%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22).jpg)

Entrance to the stadium in 1942 during the German occupation

Under [German occupation](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine") the arena became known as the **All-Ukrainian Stadium** and reopened on 12 July 1942, hosting matches of several local and German teams. Although the stadium was never directly attacked by any of the sides, it was seriously damaged during the [German retreat from Kyiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiev_(1943) "Battle of Kiev (1943)") in November 1943 and could only be reopened seven months later with a football match between [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/Dynamo_Kyiv "Dynamo Kyiv") and [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/CSKA_Moscow "CSKA Moscow"), which had originally been scheduled for the opening of the arena back in 1941.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

In the postwar years the stadium was reconstructed, receiving its first [scoreboard](/wiki/Scoreboard "Scoreboard"), its entrance portal was completed, and in 1956 four 500-[lux](/wiki/Lux "Lux") lighting masts with the height of 45 meters were installed. In October 1962 the arena became known as **Central Stadium**. In the next year a [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic")-produced electronic scoreboard appeared. Another reconstruction took place from 1966, with a second tier being added to the stadium, which allowed it to host over 100,000 spectators. Specialized rooms for commentators' booths, two new electronic scoreboards and even a [ski jump](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping") were added during that time.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

In preparation for the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics"), after 1977 the stadium underwent another reconstruction, receiving new drainage, an [Olympic cauldron](/wiki/Olympic_flame "Olympic flame"), a modernized lighting system with four 82-meter masts and various additional facilities. During that period the arena once again became known as the **Republican Stadium**.[[8]](#cite_note-vel-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Kij%C3%B3w_1993_001.jpg/250px-Kij%C3%B3w_1993_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kij%C3%B3w_1993_001.jpg)

View of the stands in early 1990s

After [Ukrainian independence](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine#Independence "History of Ukraine") in 1991, the stadium was given national status in 1996 and renamed again as the "Olympic" National Sports Complex. Kyivans still commonly refer to it as the *Tsentralny* (Central) or *Respublikanskyi stadion* (Republican Stadium); the nearby [metro](/wiki/Kyiv_Metro "Kyiv Metro") station "[Olimpiiska](/wiki/Olimpiiska_(Kyiv_Metro) "Olimpiiska (Kyiv Metro)")" was also initially called "Respublikanskyi Stadion".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BF%D0%B8%D0%B9%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BF%D0%B8%D0%B9%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9D%D0%A1%D0%9A_%22%D0%9E%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BF%D0%B8%D0%B9%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9%22_-_panoramio.jpg)

Stadium at the beginning of its latest reconstruction in 2008

In 1997–99, the stadium was renovated again in accordance with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") guidelines, and its capacity was reduced to 83,450. The stadium continued to serve as the home territory of Dynamo, with the Lobanovsky stadium serving as a training ground. Sometime after 1998, major changes took place, as it was no longer efficient to keep and maintain the stadium as a club ground. Dynamo decided to reconstruct the Lobanovsky Dynamo Stadium as its primary ground, because match attendances rarely exceeded 10,000 spectators. Since that time, Olympic has been used primarily for [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") international matches, and it was lent to [FC Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") for high-profile home games when a high attendance was expected. However, it is not the official home ground of Dynamo or any other Kyiv club, as they all have smaller home stadiums and training bases. The stadium is an official home ground of the [Ukraine national football team](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") and was the official venue of the [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Cup "Ukrainian Cup") final until 2008. In 2008, the stadium underwent a major reconstruction in preparation for the [continental championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Kiyev_Olimpiya_Stadionu.jpg/250px-Kiyev_Olimpiya_Stadionu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiyev_Olimpiya_Stadionu.jpg)

Interior of the stadium

During the [2019 Ukrainian presidential election](/wiki/2019_Ukrainian_presidential_election "2019 Ukrainian presidential election"), the debate between then-incumbent [Petro Poroshenko](/wiki/Petro_Poroshenko "Petro Poroshenko") and eventual winner [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy") was held in the Olympic Stadium.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Previous names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Olympic_Stadium_(Kyiv)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Previous names")]

For most of its history, the stadium was known as the Republican Stadium.

* 1923–1924: [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") [Red](/wiki/Red_(political_adjective) "Red (political adjective)") Stadium
* 1924–1935: Red Stadium
* 1936–1938: [Stanislav Kosior](/wiki/Stanislav_Kosior "Stanislav Kosior") [Republican](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") Stadium
* 1938–1941: Republican Stadium
* 1941: [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") Republican Stadium (50,000 capacity in 1941)
* 1941–1943: All-Ukrainian Stadium *([Occupation by Nazi Germany](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine"))*
* 1943–1962: [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") Republican Stadium (capacity reduced to 47,756 in 1944)
* 1962–1979: Central Stadium (100,062 capacity in 1967)
* 1980–1996: Republican Stadium
* 1996–present: Olympic National Sports Complex[[10]](#cite_note-history-10) (83,450 capacity in 1999; reduced to 70,050 in 2011)

## 1980 Summer Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Olympic_Stadium_(Kyiv)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1980 Summer Olympics")]

Main article: [Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

During the 1980 Summer Olympics, three [Group C](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics#Group_C "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") and three [Group D](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics#Group_D "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") matches, as well as a quarter-final, were scheduled at the stadium, for a total of seven games. In the first of these matches (held on 20 July 1980), [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_national_football_team "East Germany national football team") tied with [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") by a scoreline of 1–1. The sole quarter-final (held on 27 July 1980) saw [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_national_football_team "East Germany national football team") beat [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team") with a record-breaking score of 4–0 on the way to their third title.

View of the stadium during the [Euro 2012 final match](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_Final "UEFA Euro 2012 Final")

On 18 April 2007, Poland and Ukraine were chosen by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") to co-host the finals of [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) with the Olimpiyskiy Stadium set to host the final.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The reconstruction of the stadium involved the demolition and rebuilding of the lower tier, a completely new west stand with a two-level press box, luxury boxes between the two tiers, the addition of a 13-storey high-rise building to the west (to house the Sheraton Kyiv Olimpiysky Hotel), and the addition of a new roof (of unique design) covering the entire seating area. The capacity of the stadium after the reconstruction is 70,050. Reconstruction began on 1 December 2008, when the winner of a tender was announced.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was scheduled to be finished in 2011. The stadium was officially opened by [Ukrainian President](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine") [Viktor Yanukovych](/wiki/Viktor_Yanukovych "Viktor Yanukovych") on 8 October 2011.[[14]](#cite_note-YanuBOO-14)

The three [Group D](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2012 Group D") matches involving [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team"), a quarter-final, and the final were scheduled for here (with the other matches in Group D being played at the [Donbas Arena](/wiki/Donbas_Arena "Donbas Arena")). In the first match, Ukraine beat Sweden by a scoreline of 2–1. [The final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_Final "UEFA Euro 2012 Final"), held on 1 July 2012, saw [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") defeat [Italy](/wiki/Italian_national_football_team "Italian national football team") by the record-breaking score of 4–0 on the way to their third title.

When international music superstars or bands come to Kyiv, their concerts are often held in this stadium, as it is the biggest in Ukraine and one of the biggest in Europe. Artists who have performed here include [George Michael](/wiki/George_Michael "George Michael") and [Shakira](/wiki/Shakira "Shakira").

[The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") were scheduled to perform at the stadium on 25 July 2007 as part of their [A Bigger Bang Tour](/wiki/A_Bigger_Bang_(concert_tour) "A Bigger Bang (concert tour)"), but the concert was moved to [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Poland because of political crisis and early parliament elections in Ukraine.

On 25 July 2012 a concert of the bands [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers"), [Kasabian](/wiki/Kasabian "Kasabian") and [The Vaccines](/wiki/The_Vaccines "The Vaccines") was held in the stadium.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") performed a concert at the stadium on 4 August 2012 as part of her [The MDNA Tour](/wiki/The_MDNA_Tour "The MDNA Tour"). 31,022 people visited her show.

[Depeche Mode](/wiki/Depeche_Mode "Depeche Mode") performed at the stadium on 29 June 2013 during their [The Delta Machine Tour](/wiki/The_Delta_Machine_Tour "The Delta Machine Tour"), in front of a crowd of 36,562 people.

Famous Ukrainian rock-band [Okean Elzy](/wiki/Okean_Elzy "Okean Elzy") performed in the stadium their 20 anniversary on 21 June 2014, with an attendance of 71,045 people. The band again performed here on 18 June 2016 as part of their 2016–2017 world tour.

[Aerosmith](/wiki/Aerosmith "Aerosmith") were scheduled to perform at the stadium on 2 July 2014 as part of their [Global Warming Tour](/wiki/Global_Warming_Tour "Global Warming Tour"). However, the concert was cancelled due to the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War").

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Olympic_Stadium_(Kyiv)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transportation")]

The stadium is located right in the centre of Kyiv on the right bank of the River Dnipro. The stadium can be approached mainly by either Velyka Vasylkivska Street or Lesi Ukrainki Boulevard. Both streets' southern ends connect to the [European route E95](/wiki/European_route_E95 "European route E95"), which is known at that part of the city as Druzhby Narodiv Boulevard. However the main arena of the complex does not have a direct access to the mentioned streets and can only be reached through several smaller streets such as Fizkultury, [Saksahanskoho](/w/index.php?title=Saksahanskoho_Street&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saksahanskoho Street (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%92%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%86%D1%8F_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_(%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2) "uk:Вулиця Саксаганського (Київ)")], Shota Rustaveli, Esplanadna Streets and Hospitalny Lane.

There are several subway stations on the [Kyiv Metro](/wiki/Kyiv_Metro "Kyiv Metro") located within walking distance: "[Olimpiiska](/wiki/Olimpiiska_(Kyiv_Metro) "Olimpiiska (Kyiv Metro)")" (~300 metres (0.19 miles)) and "[Palats Sportu](/wiki/Palats_Sportu_(Kyiv_Metro) "Palats Sportu (Kyiv Metro)")" (~400 metres (0.25 miles)). These are usually closed during matches. It is possible to use other nearby stations [Klovska](/wiki/Klovska_(Kyiv_Metro) "Klovska (Kyiv Metro)"), [Zoloti Vorota](/wiki/Zoloti_Vorota_(Kyiv_Metro) "Zoloti Vorota (Kyiv Metro)"), [Teatralna](/wiki/Teatralna_(Kyiv_Metro) "Teatralna (Kyiv Metro)"), [Palats "Ukrayina"](/wiki/Palats_%22Ukrayina%22_(Kyiv_Metro) "Palats \"Ukrayina\" (Kyiv Metro)"). In December 2010, Kyiv City State Administration renamed the subway station "Respublikansky Stadion" as "Olimpiiska".[[17]](#cite_note-17)
